headline: National cooperative database
summaryText: National Cooperative Database (NCD) has been developed by Ministry of Cooperation, Government of India. The NCD portal was launched on 08th March, 2024. The National Cooperative Database is accessible at https://cooperatives.gov.in.
fullText:
National cooperative database

The National Cooperative Database (NCD) has been developed by Ministry of Cooperation, Government of India, with the support of States / UTs. The NCD portal was launched on 08th March, 2024. This database was developed in three phases, and includes data on parameters such as location, membership, economic activities, infrastructure of the cooperatives, financial performance and Audit details of cooperatives. The National Cooperative Database is accessible at https://cooperatives.gov.in.

The National Cooperative Database (NCD) provides a single-point access to information on over 8.4 lakh cooperatives across the country, having approximately 31 crore members across 30 Sectors. As per NCD, the State/UT-wise total no. of Cooperative Societies is attached as Annexure-1.

To ensure data accuracy and interoperability, the National Cooperative Database (NCD) portal is integrated with various Government portals and digital platforms such as Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Jan Aushadhi Kendras (PMBJK) database of Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, Common Service Centres (CSC) database of Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeiTY), Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samruddhi Kendras (PMKSK) database of Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, the Udyam Portal of the Ministry of Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises (MSME) and Computerization of PACS (e-PACS) portal.

Further, to maintain accurate and updated data on cooperatives, the NCD portal is also being integrated with Registrar of Cooperative Societies (RCSs) portals of all State/UTs through APIs, enabling automatic data updates, synchronization, and efficient information management. As of 01.03.2026, the States of Rajasthan, Chhattisgarh, Bihar, Mizoram and Dadra and Nagar Haveli & Daman and Diu have completed integration with the NCD portal.

The National Cooperative Database (NCD) can be used by policy makers, State Governments and other stakeholders to strengthen the cooperative movement, particularly in areas where societies are underperforming. The database is used for identification of gaps in the geographical spread of cooperatives, including both covered and uncovered gram panchayats across the country including Rajasamand district of Rajasthan.

Using the National Cooperative Database (NCD), the Government launched the Cooperative Ranking Framework on 24th January 2025 to assess and rank cooperatives, State / District / Block-wise and sector-wise. The Registrar of Cooperative Societies (RCS) of State / UT can generate the ranking of primary cooperative societies of 12 major sectors through the NCD portal. This ranking system aims to enhance transparency, reliability, and competitiveness among cooperative societies, ultimately fostering their growth.

headline: Computerisation of Registrar of Cooperative Societies Offices
summaryText: The total funds allocated under the Government of India’s share for the project are ₹82.85 crore (including Maharashtra and Uttar Pradesh) The project is in progress in 30 States/UTs and has been completed in 5. The delay in achieving the target under the Computerization of Offices of Registrar of Cooperative Societies (RCS) project is due to the following reasons.
fullText:
Computerisation of Registrar of Cooperative Societies Offices

The project is in progress in 30 States/UTs and has been completed in 5 States/UTs. The State of Kerala has not shown interest in the project.

The total funds allocated under the Government of India’s share for the project are ₹82.85 crore (including Maharashtra and Uttar Pradesh). Out of this, a total amount of ₹29.02 crore has been released to the States/UTs so far. Under the project, the States/UTs have utilized approximately ₹18 crore for activities such as procurement of hardware, development of software, and other implementation-related requirements for the computerization of the offices of the Registrar of Cooperative Societies (RCS).

The delay in achieving the target under the Computerization of Offices of Registrar of Cooperative Societies (RCS) of States/UTs (including Jharkhand) project is due to the following reasons: -

Paucity of time during the initial phase of implementation, as only a limited period was available for execution, and many States/UTs were not ready with the mapping of their SNA accounts with the Public Financial Management System (PFMS).

Introduction of the new CSNA model in PFMS for the disbursement of funds and delays in mapping States/UTs as child agencies under the system.

Delay by several States/UTs in utilizing the released funds due to pending administrative approvals for projects, non-finalization of vendors for software development, delays in hardware procurement, and delay in release of matching grants by the States/UTs, resulting in non-release of subsequent instalments.

Further procedural changes, such as the introduction of the SNA SPARSH model and the Treasury Single Account (TSA) model, under which RBI accounts of States/UTs are required to be opened at the regional branch of the Reserve Bank of India in Delhi, which required considerable time to complete the necessary formalities.

States have been actively engaged to ensure the smooth and timely implementation of the initiatives of the Ministry. Monthly meetings are organised under the chairmanship of Secretary Cooperation through video conferencing with the States/UTs to review the progress of initiatives undertaken by the Ministry of Cooperation. In addition, quarterly review meetings are conducted in physical mode with the Principal Secretaries/Secretaries of the Cooperative Departments of the States and the Registrars of Cooperative Societies of the States/UTs. Recently, the ‘Manthan Baithak’ was held on 17th February 2026 under the chairmanship of the Hon’ble Union Home and Cooperation Minister, with the participation of the Hon’ble Cooperation Ministers of States/UTs.

The implementation of the project by the majority of the States/UTs is currently in progress. A few States/UTs have only recently completed the project; therefore, it is too early to assess the measurable improvements in the functioning of the RCS offices.

The Ministry does not maintain district-level data on the Computerization of Primary Agriculture Credit Societies (PACS). However, out of the total sanctioned PACS of 6,257 in Uttar Pradesh, 3,057 PACS have been computerized. The count of cooperative societies registered in the National Cooperative Database (NCD), state-wise and district-wise (including Bulandshahr district of Uttar Pradesh), is enclosed in Annexure I.

As the implementation of the projects is currently in progress in the majority of States/UTs, it is too early to assess the impact of these initiatives on transparency, compliance efficiency, and service delivery in the cooperative sector.

headline: Launching of Bharat Taxi
summaryText: Bharat Taxi is India’s first cooperative-led ride-hailing platform. The platform aims to provide livelihood and self-employment opportunities, especially for driver-owners, unemployed people, and youth.
fullText:
The Ministry of Cooperation is committed to empowering cooperatives as instruments of employment generation, social security, and grassroots economic participation. Bharat Taxi is envisaged as a transformative intervention in the mobility sector by placing drivers—referred to as Sarathis—at the centre of ownership, governance, and value creation, thereby offering a sustainable and dignified alternative to aggregator-driven models. “Bharat Taxi” is India’s first cooperative-led ride-hailing platform. This is a major milestone in the Government of India’s ongoing efforts to strengthen the cooperative sector and promote inclusive, citizen-centric mobility solutions, in line with the vision of ‘Sahkar se Samriddhi’. Registered under the Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act, 2002, Bharat Taxi was established on 6 June 2025 by 8 national level institutions working in field of cooperatives and officially launched on 5th February, 2026.

As of now the Bharat Taxi service is functional in Delhi NCR – Delhi, Gurugram, Noida, Faridabad, Ghaziabad and in Ahmedabad, Rajkot, Somnath & Dwarka of Gujarat. Bharat Taxi targets to expand its service in a phased manner across the country over the next two years.

Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) have been signed by Bharat Taxi with Paytm, Airports Authority of India, Delhi Metro Rail Corporation, Delhi Traffic Police, National e-Governance Division, Delhi Tourism and Transportation Development Corporation, IFFCO Tokio General Insurance, GMR Group, and State Bank of India. These partnerships are intended to facilitate collaboration across multiple areas, including digital payments, airport and metro connectivity, traffic and transport coordination, e-governance systems, tourism-related travel facilitation, insurance coverage, infrastructure integration, and institutional banking support. Collectively, these initiatives are expected to contribute to the development of a more coordinated and citizen-friendly mobility ecosystem.

The platform aims to provide livelihood and self-employment opportunities, especially for driver-owners, unemployed people, and youth, through a cooperative model. It encourages inclusive growth by strengthens local economies. The initiative is expected to create both direct and indirect employment opportunities. As it grows, it will engage various stakeholders in implementation, logistics, community engagement, and service delivery, thereby creating avenues for livelihood and self-employment, depending on the scale of participation and the pace of its growth.

The Bharat Taxi platform operates on zero-commission model, with direct distribution of profits to drivers, offering a homegrown and indigenous alternative to investment-driven aggregator platforms. Bharat Taxi has transparent fare structure, a user-friendly mobile ride-booking interface, real-time vehicle tracking, and support for multilingual interfaces, along with 24/7 customer support to enhance accessibility. The platform also provides secure and verified onboarding promoting inclusive mobility.

Bharat Taxi has launched women-focused initiatives such as ‘Bike Didi’ and ‘Sarthi Didi’ to promote greater participation of women in India’s first cooperative ride-hailing service. Women’s participation is further encouraged through targeted outreach, skill development and training, financial assistance, safety measures, and preferential onboarding, along with collaboration with women self-help groups and cooperative institutions.

This information was given by Union Minister for Home and Cooperation Shri Amit Shah in a written reply in Lok Sabha.

headline: Multi state cooperative credit societies
summaryText: Multi-State Cooperative Societies (MSCS) Act, 2002 function as autonomous cooperative organisations. Notices were sent to these Societies for seeking objections to initiate winding up process. No satisfactory reply was received from these Soc societies; therefore, winding up orders were passed. Liquidators were appointed under section 89 of MSCS Act,2002 in these Society.
fullText:
Multi state cooperative credit societies

Cooperative Societies registered under the provisions of the Multi-State Cooperative Societies (MSCS) Act, 2002 function as autonomous cooperative organisations and are accountable to their members. MSCSs function as per the provisions of Multi-State Cooperative Societies Act, 2002 and rules framed there under read with approved bye-laws of the Society which includes the role and powers of the member, Board, General Body of the Society and Central Registrar of Cooperative Societies (CRCS). As per the provisions of Section 49 of the MSCS Act, 2002, the business matters such as to admit members, to accept the deposits and refund to depositors etc., fall under the powers and functions of the board of the Society and the day to day management of the Society fall under the powers and functions of the Chief Executive of the Society as per the provisions of Section 52 of the MSCS Act, 2002.

Based on the various complaints received against Jijau Maa Saheb Multi State Cooperative Credit Society Ltd., Dnyanaradha Multi State Cooperative Credit Society Ltd., Shubh Kalyan Multi State Cooperative Credit Society Ltd., Shri Sairam Urban Multistate Cooperative Credit Society Ltd., and Rajasthani Multi State Cooperative Credit Society Ltd., the Central Registrar of Cooperative Societies has requested Registrar of Cooperative Societies, Government of Maharashtra to conduct the inspection u/s 108 of the Multi-State Cooperative Societies (MSCS) Act, 2002. Based on Inspection Reports, Notices were sent to these Societies for seeking objections to initiate winding up process under the provision of MSCS Act, 2002. No satisfactory reply was received from these Societies; therefore, winding up orders u/s 86 of the MSCS Act, 2002 were passed and liquidators were appointed under section 89 of MSCS Act, 2002 in these Societies.

The Liquidator has to liquidate the assets of Society and to ensure payment to investors/members under the powers of the liquidator entrusted to him as per the provisions of Section 90 of the MSCS Act, 2002 and by adopting procedures as per Rule 28 and 29 of the MSCS Rules, 2002 in a time bound manner.

This information was given by Union Minister for Home and Cooperation Shri Amit Shah in a written reply in Lok Sabha.

headline: SCHOLARSHIP TO SC COMMUNITY
summaryText: The eligibility criteria for SC and ST students for these schemes is as under. Out of 2000 slots annually, 1500 slots for UGC-NET (for humanities) and 500 slots for joint UGC/CSIR NET (for science stream) have been earmarked. The State/UT-wise details of SC/ST beneficiaries during the last three years are at Annexure- I.
fullText:
Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment

SCHOLARSHIP TO SC COMMUNITY

The Department of Social Justice and Empowerment and the Ministry of Tribal Affairs are implementing the ‘National Fellowship Scheme for Scheduled Caste Students’ (NFSC) and the ‘National Fellowship Scheme for Scheduled Tribe Students’ (NFST) for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes Ph.D. scholars respectively.

The eligibility criteria for SC and ST students for these schemes is as under:

National Fellowship Scheme for Scheduled Caste Students (NFSC)

(i)Qualifying UGC-NET (JRF) or Joint UGC-CSIR NET examination and securing admission in Universities/Institutions recognized by the University Grants Commission (UGC).

National Fellowship Scheme for Scheduled Tribe Students (NFST)

(i) At least 55% marks in Post-Graduation and qualifying UGC-NET or Joint UGC-CSIR NET examination and securing admission in Universities/Institutions covered under the scheme.

Under National Fellowship Scheme for Scheduled Caste Students (NFSC) and National Fellowship Scheme for Scheduled Tribe Students (NFST), there are no separate categories of scholarships/fellowships. However, under the NFSC scheme, out of 2000 slots annually, 1500 slots for UGC-NET (for humanities) and 500 slots for joint UGC-CSIR NET (for science stream) have been earmarked.

The State/UT-wise details of SC and ST beneficiaries during the last three years are at Annexure- I

To enhance access, awareness, and timely disbursement of scholarships to SC/ST Ph.D. scholars, the Government has undertaken several measures such as implementation of the schemes through a dedicated online Scholarship and Fellowship Management Portal (SFMP), disbursement of fellowships through Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) in the aadhaar seeded account of the eligible applicants, streamlining of verification processes and dissemination of information through the websites and outreach activities of the Department, NSFDC and UGC to ensure wider awareness among eligible students.
